title: Everyday wording vs formal wording
text: Formal register replaces casual wording with more precise professional phrasing. The meaning should stay the same while the tone becomes more exact and professional.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace casual wording with a more formal synonym or to express an idea in more formal English.

examples.text: get approval -> obtain approval
help me with this task -> assist me with this task
He is really good at his job. -> He demonstrates exceptional professional competence.

avoid: Do not frame this as a tense or agreement error when the learner's wording is grammatical but too casual.
Do not introduce unrelated advice about punctuation or sentence length.

title: General wording vs precise synonym
text: These drills ask for a more exact synonym, not just a fancier word. The replacement should sharpen the meaning without changing the basic idea of the sentence.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks for a more advanced or more precise synonym in a sentence-level substitution item.

examples.text: big -> substantial
use the correct procedure -> utilize the correct procedure
That was a good decision. -> That was a sound decision.

avoid: Do not describe the task as a formal-register rewrite if the main issue is lexical precision.
Do not say any strong synonym is acceptable if it shifts the meaning.

title: Loose phrase vs single precise verb
text: These items compress a longer phrase into one stronger verb. The answer should keep the same idea while becoming more concise and exact.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace a bracketed phrase with a single precise verb.

examples.text: talked about the risks -> addressed the risks
did well in the match -> excelled in the match
worked hard to improve her technique -> refined her technique

avoid: Do not explain this as a broad style preference if the core contrast is phrase compression into one verb.
Do not praise a phrasal paraphrase as sufficient when the drill explicitly asks for a single verb.

title: Active voice vs passive voice
text: Passive voice makes the receiver of the action the subject, while active voice places the doer in the subject position. The transformation should preserve the same event and participants.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to transform a sentence into passive voice or back into active voice.

examples.text: The committee reviewed the proposal. -> The proposal was reviewed by the committee.
Mistakes were made by the team during the rollout. -> The team made mistakes during the rollout.

avoid: Do not explain this as a formal-register issue unless the prompt explicitly asks for formality.
Do not introduce tense changes unless the original tense must be preserved in the transformation.

title: Two clauses vs participle clause
text: A participle clause combines related actions into one more compact sentence. The first action becomes a modifying phrase that leads into the main clause.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to combine two sentences using a participle or participle clause.

examples.text: She studied the data. She reached a conclusion. -> Having studied the data, she reached a conclusion.

avoid: Do not explain this as a relative-clause task; the key contrast is a participle opener.
Do not say the second sentence should be deleted; it should be absorbed into a combined structure.

title: Two sentences vs relative clause
text: A relative clause attaches extra information to a noun so two related sentences become one. The sentence keeps the same subject reference while embedding the extra detail.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to combine sentences using a relative clause such as who, which, or that.

examples.text: The manager approved the budget. She leads the project. -> The manager who leads the project approved the budget.

avoid: Do not explain this as a participle-clause task; the defining contrast is noun modification with a relative clause.
Do not shift the referent of the relative pronoun.

title: Casual sentence vs formal rewrite
text: These drills keep the same core meaning but restate the sentence in a more formal tone. The answer often uses less conversational verbs and more abstract professional phrasing.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite a casual sentence in formal register or formal style.

examples.text: I think you should probably reconsider this. -> I would recommend reconsidering this course of action.
We cannot do this because we do not have enough money. -> This is not feasible due to insufficient funding.
We added a new feature. -> A new feature was implemented and deployed.

avoid: Do not call the learner broadly incorrect if the meaning is right but the tone remains too casual.
Do not focus on punctuation or article usage if the main contrast is register.

title: Wordy formal phrasing vs concise formal phrasing
text: Some formal rewrites are not about sounding more complex; they are about becoming more direct while staying formal. The best answer trims unnecessary wording but preserves a polished tone.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks for a more concise formal rewrite rather than simply a more advanced paraphrase.

examples.text: Due to the fact that the deadline was missed, we lost the contract. -> Because the deadline was missed, the contract was lost.

avoid: Do not assume longer automatically means better for formal style.
Do not explain this as a passive-voice drill unless the prompt explicitly centers voice.

title: Conversational phrase vs formal phrase
text: These items restate an informal phrase in more polished workplace or academic English. The core meaning stays intact, but the wording becomes less conversational.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to express a short everyday phrase in more formal English.

examples.text: Let's talk about this later. -> I suggest we revisit this matter at a later time.
Can you look into that? -> Could you investigate that matter?
We need to figure this out. -> We must resolve this matter.

avoid: Do not treat this as a vocabulary-only synonym swap if the full phrase is being rephrased.
Do not reduce it to grammar correction when the goal is tone and phrasing.

title: Domain commentary style vs everyday wording
text: Some items ask for a domain-specific formal tone, such as sports commentary or technical documentation. The explanation should focus on how the register shifts to match that domain, not just on vocabulary difficulty.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks for formal commentary style, technical documentation style, or another clearly domain-shaped register shift.

examples.text: He ran really fast to the finish line. -> He sprinted decisively to the finish line.
The system broke because of too much traffic. -> The system failure was attributed to excessive traffic load.
We messed up the launch. -> The launch was executed with significant deficiencies.

avoid: Do not collapse domain-specific register into a generic synonym explanation.
Do not explain the response as purely grammatical when the key contrast is tone and domain convention.

title: Casual workplace language vs formal business language
text: Formal business communication replaces casual reporting with professional terminology and impersonal constructions. Contracted forms, informal verbs, and hedging language are replaced with precise institutional phrasing.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite a casual workplace statement in formal business register — for reports, email threads, or professional documentation.

examples.text: We didn't hit our targets this quarter. -> We fell short of our projected targets this quarter.
She basically runs the whole company. -> She effectively oversees the entire organization.
We need to sort this out quickly. -> The matter requires prompt resolution.

avoid: Do not treat this as a grammar error when the language is grammatical but too casual for a business context.
Do not redirect to punctuation or article errors when the main contrast is register.

title: Everyday language vs academic register
text: Academic writing avoids first-person opinion, hedges claims impersonally, and uses abstract formal vocabulary. Colloquial phrasing is replaced with scholarly equivalents that signal objectivity and precision.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to paraphrase or express an idea in academic style or scholarly formal English.

examples.text: Everyone knows this doesn't work. -> It is widely acknowledged that this approach is ineffective.
The results prove this theory is right. -> The findings support this theoretical framework.
We think students learn better this way. -> Evidence suggests this pedagogical approach may yield superior outcomes.

avoid: Do not collapse academic register into generic formal business tone — academic style prefers impersonal hedging over assertive professional phrasing.
Do not suggest that longer sentences always improve academic register.

title: Casual money talk vs formal financial language
text: Financial and business reporting uses precise institutional vocabulary: revenue, expenditure, fiscal, deficit, portfolio. Casual money references must shift to this register in financial contexts.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite casual statements about money, budgets, or financial results in formal financial language.

examples.text: We lost a lot of money this year. -> The organization reported substantial financial losses this fiscal year.
We are spending too much. -> Expenditure has exceeded the allocated budget.

avoid: Do not reduce this to a synonym swap when the full sentence structure should shift to match financial reporting conventions.
Do not conflate this with a generic formal register note when the domain is specifically financial.

title: Everyday health language vs formal medical language
text: Medical and clinical communication uses precise terminology for symptoms, conditions, and procedures. Informal descriptions of health states should be replaced with clinical or formal health vocabulary.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite casual health or medical statements in formal clinical or professional health language.

examples.text: I have a really bad headache. -> I am experiencing severe cephalic pain.
The patient got better faster than expected. -> The patient's recovery exceeded the anticipated prognosis.

avoid: Do not collapse medical register into generic formal style — the key contrast is clinical terminology, not just politeness.
Do not introduce unrelated grammar rules when the main contrast is vocabulary precision.

title: Casual observation vs formal scientific description
text: Scientific writing replaces casual observations with precise, passive, and impersonal constructions. Findings are reported objectively without personal pronouns, and technical terminology replaces everyday language.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to express a casual observation or finding in formal scientific or research language.

examples.text: The plants grew a lot more with the extra light. -> Plant growth increased significantly under elevated light conditions.
We found that the drug worked better on older patients. -> Analysis indicated greater drug efficacy in the older patient cohort.

avoid: Do not treat this as a standard formal register note — the specific contrast is scientific objectivity and passivization, not just professional vocabulary.
Do not introduce first-person hedging in scientific descriptions; scientific formal style uses impersonal constructions.

title: Casual food description vs formal culinary language
text: Culinary and hospitality writing uses precise, elevated vocabulary to describe food, preparation, and dining experience. Common descriptions are replaced with culinary terminology and formal register.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite casual food descriptions in formal culinary or hospitality language.

examples.text: The soup was really tasty. -> The soup was exceptionally well-seasoned and flavourful.
We cooked the fish until it was done. -> The fish was prepared until fully cooked through.

avoid: Do not collapse culinary register into generic formal style — precision in ingredients and techniques is the key contrast.
Do not focus on grammar errors when the main task is vocabulary elevation.

title: Informal work phrase vs formal workplace alternative
text: These items restate an informal work communication phrase in polished professional English. The tone should be respectful, clear, and appropriate for formal written or spoken workplace contexts.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rephrase an informal workplace phrase in formal business English, especially for meetings, written communications, or professional conversations.

examples.text: That's a great idea. -> That is an excellent proposition.
We're still on the same page about this, right? -> I would like to confirm that we remain aligned on this matter.
Can you get back to me on that? -> I would appreciate a response at your earliest convenience.

avoid: Do not treat this as a grammar-only correction when the whole phrase needs reworking for register.
Do not confuse this with a vocabulary synonym task when the entire phrase structure is being rephrased.

title: Informal sport phrase vs formal sports commentary phrase
text: Sports journalism and commentary use formal, elevated phrases to describe athletic effort, performance, and outcomes. Casual sport phrases should shift to commentary register when the context calls for it.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to express a casual sport phrase in formal sports commentary or journalism language.

examples.text: The player played really well. -> The athlete delivered an outstanding performance.

avoid: Do not reduce this to a simple vocabulary swap when the entire phrase needs to shift to commentary register.
Do not conflate with domain formal sentence rewrites — this note is for phrase-level sport expressions.

title: Informal health phrase vs formal medical phrase
text: Healthcare communication uses precise, clinical phrasing when discussing conditions, treatments, and outcomes. Informal health phrases should shift to clinical or professional health language when formality is required.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to restate an informal health or medical phrase in formal clinical language.

examples.text: I don't feel well today. -> I am experiencing discomfort and reduced physical wellbeing today.

avoid: Do not collapse health phrase rephrasing into a general formal-register note — clinical vocabulary precision is the key contrast.
Do not introduce grammar corrections when the goal is health register formality.

title: Casual money phrase vs formal financial phrase
text: Financial communication uses institutional vocabulary to discuss budgets, profits, losses, and transactions. Casual money phrases should be replaced with precise financial terminology in formal contexts.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to express a casual phrase about money or finances in formal financial language.

examples.text: We are losing money. -> The organization is experiencing a revenue deficit.

avoid: Do not collapse finance phrase rephrasing into a general formal phrase note — financial terminology is the key contrast.
Do not focus on grammar when the goal is shifting to financial register.

title: Vague adjective vs precise adjective
text: These items ask the learner to replace a vague or weak adjective with one that sharply describes the noun's specific quality. The replacement should be more precise and expressive, not just more formal.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace a vague adjective (like bad, good, big, nice) with a more precise or descriptive alternative.

examples.text: The plan has a [bad] flaw. -> The plan has a critical flaw.
The team had a [big] win. -> The team had a decisive win.

avoid: Do not describe this as a formal-register task when the main contrast is adjective precision, not tone.
Do not accept any strong adjective as correct if it doesn't sharpen the specific quality described.

title: Generic sport description vs precise sports term
text: Sports vocabulary has exact terms for athletic actions, positions, and outcomes. Generic descriptions should be replaced with the sport-specific term that names the action or result precisely.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace a general word or phrase with the specific sport or athletic term for that action or outcome.

examples.text: The player [moved] quickly around the defender. -> The player dribbled quickly around the defender.
The team [got more points] in the second half. -> The team outscored their opponents in the second half.
The runner finished the race [well]. -> The runner completed the race with a personal best.

avoid: Do not explain this as a formal-register task — the core contrast is domain-specific vocabulary, not tone.
Do not accept a paraphrase when the drill asks for the exact sport term.

title: Generic description vs precise technical term
text: Technical vocabulary has exact terms for system states, operations, and concepts. Generic or informal descriptions should be replaced with the technical term that names the concept precisely.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace a general word or phrase with the correct technical or software term for that concept.

examples.text: The app [broke]. -> The application failed.
The code [stopped working] unexpectedly. -> The process crashed unexpectedly.
The system [checks] the user's identity. -> The system authenticates the user's identity.
The server [saved] the data. -> The server persisted the data.

avoid: Do not explain this as a formal-register task when the core contrast is technical term precision.
Do not accept an informal synonym when the drill asks for the specific technical vocabulary.

title: Everyday work word vs precise business term
text: Professional and business contexts have exact vocabulary for organizational roles, processes, and interactions. Everyday work descriptions should be replaced with the precise professional term that names the concept accurately.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace an everyday work word or phrase with the specific professional or business term.

examples.text: a [meeting] with the client -> a consultation with the client
the [information] was shared -> the data was disseminated
the [boss] approved it -> the line manager approved it

avoid: Do not conflate this with a general synonym precision task — the contrast is professional domain vocabulary, not just formality.
Do not accept a formal-sounding word that doesn't accurately describe the specific work concept.

title: Common everyday word vs advanced precise synonym
text: Advanced vocabulary drills ask the learner to replace a common word with a more precise or elevated synonym that fits the same context. The replacement should preserve meaning while improving lexical sophistication. In academic contexts, this means choosing the established scholarly term over a general everyday equivalent.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks for an advanced synonym in a vocabulary-level item — everyday content, academic terminology, or educational vocabulary — where the goal is lexical range and precision, not domain-specific formal register.

examples.text: start (verb) -> initiate
end (verb) -> conclude
change (verb) -> modify
look at (verb) -> examine
test (noun, academic) -> assessment
school essay (noun) -> academic composition

avoid: Do not explain this as a formal-register task if the item is standalone vocabulary without a sentence context.
Do not accept a synonym that sounds advanced but shifts the meaning.
Do not say the learner's answer is wrong if the app already marked it correct — variants like 'examination' for 'assessment' can be valid.

title: Two sentences vs non-restrictive relative clause
text: A non-restrictive relative clause adds extra, parenthetical information about a noun. It uses a comma before 'which' or 'who' and can be removed without changing the core meaning. This is distinct from a restrictive clause, which identifies which noun is meant.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to combine two sentences using a non-restrictive relative clause (comma + which or who).

examples.text: The report was very detailed. It was submitted on Monday. -> The report, which was submitted on Monday, was very detailed.
My colleague finished the project. She has ten years of experience. -> My colleague, who has ten years of experience, finished the project.

avoid: Do not explain this as a restrictive relative clause — the defining contrast is the comma and the non-essential nature of the added information.
Do not omit the commas; they are required for non-restrictive clauses.

title: Casual conditional vs formal inverted conditional
text: Formal English often inverts the conditional structure instead of using 'if': 'Should you need help' instead of 'If you need help', or 'Were this to occur' instead of 'If this occurred'. This inversion raises the register significantly.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite a casual 'if' conditional in a formal inverted conditional structure.

examples.text: If you need assistance, please contact us. -> Should you require assistance, please do not hesitate to contact us.
If the situation changes, we will inform you. -> Should the situation change, we will notify you accordingly.

avoid: Do not explain inverted conditionals as a generic formal register rewrite — the specific structure being taught is the subject-auxiliary inversion.
Do not use both 'if' and the inversion in the same answer — they are alternatives.

title: Verb phrase vs nominalized form
text: Nominalization converts verb phrases into abstract noun phrases. This pattern is common in formal and academic writing and gives writing an impersonal, structured tone ('the implementation of' instead of 'we implemented').
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to convert an active verb phrase into a nominalized or abstract noun form, or when the expected answer uses a nominalized structure.

examples.text: We decided to postpone the meeting. -> A decision was made to postpone the meeting.
The engineers improved the system. -> An improvement to the system was achieved.

avoid: Do not explain nominalization as simply using passive voice — the key contrast is the shift from verbal to nominal construction.
Do not suggest nominalization always improves clarity; note that it trades directness for formality.

title: Casual sport report vs formal sport reporting language
text: Formal sports reporting uses an elevated, journalistic register with precise action verbs and measured language. Casual match descriptions must shift to this register when the context is formal commentary or sports journalism.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite a casual sport description in formal sports reporting or journalism style.

examples.text: The team played really well together. -> The squad demonstrated exceptional cohesion throughout the fixture.

avoid: Do not collapse this into a generic formal rewrite — sports journalism has its own distinct vocabulary and construction patterns.
Do not confuse this with the domain commentary note, which covers both sport and tech domain registers.

title: Informal education language vs formal academic register
text: Academic and educational writing uses formal register to describe learning outcomes, research processes, and institutional practices. Colloquial descriptions of teaching or study should shift to the language of educational discourse.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to rewrite a casual education or learning statement in formal academic or institutional language.

examples.text: Students get better grades when they study more. -> Academic achievement correlates positively with increased study time.
The teacher told the students to work in groups. -> The instructor directed the students to engage in collaborative learning activities.

avoid: Do not collapse this into the general academic register note — the specific context is educational institutions and pedagogical practices.
Do not treat informal phrasing as grammatically wrong when the task is register elevation.

title: Everyday money word vs precise financial term
text: Financial contexts have exact vocabulary for monetary flows, obligations, and positions. Everyday descriptions of money should be replaced with the precise financial or accounting term that names the concept accurately.
when_to_use: Use when the drill asks the learner to replace an everyday money word or phrase with the specific financial or accounting term for that concept.

examples.text: [money coming in] -> revenue / income
[money going out] -> expenditure / expenses
[debt] -> liability

avoid: Do not conflate this with a formal-register task — the contrast is domain-specific financial vocabulary, not tone.
Do not accept a general synonym when the drill asks for the specific financial term.
